coredata.dc:title: High-bright fluorescent carbon dot as versatile sensing platform
coredata.dc:creator: Han, Mumei
Wang, Liping
Li, Siheng
Bai, Liang
Zhou, Yunjie
Sun, Yue
Huang, Hui
Li, Hao
Liu, Yang
Kang, Zhenhui
coredata.dc:description: The surface functionalization will introduce additional functional groups on carbon dots (CDs) surface and then enrich the properties of CDs. Here, we show the various surface functionalized CDs (-COOH, -OH, -SH, -NH2, etc, named as NS-CDs) were synthesized with fascinating features, including high quantum efficiency (38.9%), long-term stability and good biocompatibility. Notably, it can serve as multifunction fluorescent probe in sensing system, including label-free detections in hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) with a wide linear range (1.20 × 10−3 – 8.80 × 10–12 M) and a low limit of detection (LOD, 1.00 × 10–12 M); and glutathione, covering a concentration range of 2.00 × 10−3 – 1.00 × 10−7 M and LOD of 1.00 × 10−7 M. In addition, the NS-CDs as fluorescent probe could selectively detect metal ions (such as, Hg2+, 1.00 × 10−8 − 1.50 × 10−3 M, 1.00 × 10−7 M), antibiotics (tetracycline, 1.00 ×10−10 − 2.50 × 10−5 M, 1.00 ×10−10 M) and toxic pollutant (nitrobenzene, 5.00 × 10−7 to 1.00 × 10−3 gL−1, 5.00 × 10−7 gL−1) with wide linear range and satisfactory detection limits.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Surface functionalization
Fluorescent probe
Versatile sensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of various functional groups of CDs
Calculation of the fluorescence quantum yield
Detection of H2O2 with fluorescence quenching and detection of Glutathione with fluorescence enhancement
Detections of heavy metal ions (Hg2+, Ag+)

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by the Collaborative Innovation Center of Suzhou Nano Science and Technology, the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51422207, 51572179, 21471106, 21501126), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20161216) and a project funded by the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) TEM image of NS-CDs. Inset is the size distribution of NS-CDs. b) HRTEM image of one NS-CD particle. c) UV–vis absorption spectrum and FT-IR spectrum of NS-CDs (black and red traces, respectively). d) Fluorescence emission spectra of NS-CDs with different excitation wavelengths. Insets are the digital photos of NS-CDs under visible and UV (365nm) light.
a) Full survey XPS spectrum, high-resolution XPS spectra b) C 1s, c) N 1s, and d) S 2p of NS-CDs.
Normalized fluorescence intensity of NS-CDs against a) pH value and b) ionic strength in NaCl aqueous solution.

a) PL spectra of NS-CDs aqueous solution upon addition of a series of high concentration of H2O2 from 1.42 × 10−7 to 3.85 × 10−2 mol / L. b) The relationship between I/I0 and H2O2 concentration (25°C, pH = 7.0, incubation time 20min, I and I0 are the PL intensity of the NS-CDs in the presence and absence of H2O2). c) PL spectra of NS-CDs aqueous solution upon addition of a series of low concentration of H2O2 from 8.82 × 10–12 to 2.92 × 10−7 mol / L. d) The relationship between I/ I0 and H2O2 concentration. (25°C, pH = 70, incubation time 20min, I and I0 are the PL intensity of the NS-CDs in the presence and absence of H2O2).

a) Fluorescence emission spectra of NS-CDs, NS-CDs/GSH, NS-CDs/H2O2 solutions (Quenching), and NS-CDs/H2O2 system with 0.1μmol / L GSH (Recovering). b) The corresponding digital photographs of NS-CDs, NS-CDs/H2O2 and NS-CDs/H2O2 with 0.1μmolL−1 GSH solution under white and UV (365nm) light, respectively. c) Fluorescence spectra of NS-CDs/H2O2 system with enhanced fluorescence using various concentration of GSH from 0 to 3.57 × 10−8 mol / L. d) The relationship between I/I0 and GSH concentration (25°C, pH = 7.0, incubation time 20min, I and I0 are the PL intensity of the NS-CDs/H2O2 system in the presence and absence of GSH).
Schematic illustration of fluorescence quenching and recovering mechanism in the NS-CDs/H2O2/GSH system.

a) The effect of potential interfering substances on the NS-CDs fluorescence intensity. b) The comparison of detection of H2O2 with the fluorescence probe (NS-CDs) and Titration method. c) The comparison of detection of GSH with NS-CDs/H2O2 system and HPLC method.

a) The Fluorescence spectra of NS-CDs in the presence of different Hg2+ concentrations solutions (from 0 to 2.25 × 10−3 mol / L). b) The relationship between I/I0 and Hg2+ concentration, (25°C, pH = 7.0, incubation time 20min). c) The Fluorescence spectra of NS-CDs in the presence of different Ag+ concentrations (from 0 to 2.08 × 10−4 mol / L). d) The relationship between I/I0 and Ag+ concentration, (25°C, pH = 7.0, incubation time 20min).

a) The Fluorescence spectra of NS-CDs in the presence of different NB concentrations solutions (from 0 to 1.35mg / L). b) The relationship between I/I0 and NB concentration (25°C, pH = 7.0, incubation time 20min).

a) The Fluorescence spectra of NS-CDs in the presence of different TC concentrations solutions (from 0 to 4.60 × 10−4 mol / L). b) The relationship between I/I0 and TC concentration (25°C, pH = 7.0, incubation time 20min). c) The Fluorescence spectra of NS-CDs in the presence of different EH concentrations (from 0 to 9.47 ×10−4 mol / L). d) The relationship between I/I0 and EH concentration (25°C, pH = 7.0, incubation time 20min).

head.ce:title: High-bright fluorescent carbon dot as versatile sensing platform

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-based Functional Materials and Devices, Institute of Functional Nano and Soft Materials (FUNSOM), Soochow University, Suzhou 215123, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-based Functional Materials and Devices, Institute of Functional Nano and Soft Materials (FUNSOM), Soochow University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical Engineering, National University of Singapore, Singapore 117576, Singapore

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Mechanical Engineering, National University of Singapore

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The surface functionalization will introduce additional functional groups on carbon dots (CDs) surface and then enrich the properties of CDs. Here, we show the various surface functionalized CDs (-COOH, -OH, -SH, -NH2, etc, named as NS-CDs) were synthesized with fascinating features, including high quantum efficiency (38.9%), long-term stability and good biocompatibility. Notably, it can serve as multifunction fluorescent probe in sensing system, including label-free detections in hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) with a wide linear range (1.20 × 10−3 – 8.80 × 10–12 M) and a low limit of detection (LOD, 1.00 × 10–12 M); and glutathione, covering a concentration range of 2.00 × 10−3 – 1.00 × 10−7 M and LOD of 1.00 × 10−7 M. In addition, the NS-CDs as fluorescent probe could selectively detect metal ions (such as, Hg2+, 1.00 × 10−8 − 1.50 × 10−3 M, 1.00 × 10−7 M), antibiotics (tetracycline, 1.00 ×10−10 − 2.50 × 10−5 M, 1.00 ×10−10 M) and toxic pollutant (nitrobenzene, 5.00 × 10−7 to 1.00 × 10−3 gL−1, 5.00 × 10−7 gL−1) with wide linear range and satisfactory detection limits.

We show various surface functionalized CDs (-COOH, -OH, -SH, -NH, etc) with fascinating features can be label-free detection in hydrogen peroxide, glutathione, heavy metal ions (Hg2+/Ag+), antibiotics and toxic pollutant with satisfactory detection limits and range.
fx1

ce:section.ce:para: Over the past ten years, there has been tremendous progress in the study of carbon dot (CDs) [1]. Recently, CDs with fascinating properties have become a rising star due to their benign, abundant and inexpensive nature. Compared to traditional semiconductor quantum dots and organic dyes, photoluminescent (PL) CDs are superior in terms of high (aqueous) solubility, robust chemical inertness, facile modification and high resistance to photobleaching [2,3]. The superior biological properties, such as low toxicity and good biocompatibility, render CDs with potential applications in bioimaging, [4] biosensor [5] and biomolecule delivery. [6] The outstanding electrochemical properties of CDs endow them with great potentials in optics, catalysis and sensors [7]. Besides, the excellent PL and photochemical properties of CDs make them to be efficient catalysts (such as, photocatalysts for selective oxidation, [8] light-driven acid-catalysis and hydrogen bond catalysis [9]) and very active additives commonly used in energy devices (solar cell, [10] photo electronic water splitting cell, [11] battery and super-capacitors [12,13]). Unfortunately, no one has yet systematically studied the effect of surface functional groups engineering of CDs on the versatile requirements for biological sensor and detection.

The determination of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) plays an important role in many fields. To detect H2O2, numerous analytical techniques have been reported, including optical (absorption, chemiluminescence, and spectrometry) [15,16] and electrochemical (titrimetry) [14,17–20], among which they usually suffered from the low sensitivity, poor specificity, time-consuming operating process and expensive reagents. Therefore, it is imperative to develop rapid, sensitive, specific and cost-effective approaches for the detection of H2O2. Glutathione (GSH) is also of crucial in biological systems, which contains abundant of thiol. A number of methods have been developed to determine GSH, such as electrochemistry, [21] spectrometry, [22] enzymatic assays, [23] high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) and gas chromatography (GC). [24] Heavy metals pollution and antibiotics, such as tetracycline and enrofloxacin hydrochloride, have always been global concerns. A lot of sensors have been developed to monitor these harmful substances, which can be divided into optical, [25] and electrochemical sensors [26]. Among the above analytic approaches, spectrofluorimetry is an accurate, sensitive, and cost-effective method.

In this work, we report the various functional groups (-COOH, -OH, -SH, -NH2, etc) on the surfaces of CDs, and further tested the functionalized CDs (Denote as NS-CDs) as multi-function fluorescent probes for versatile sensing, including label-free detections in hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) with a wide linear range (1.20 × 10−3 − 8.80 × 10–12 M) and a low limit of detection (LOD, 1.00 × 10–12 M); and glutathione, covering a concentration range of 2.00 × 10−3 – 1.00 × 10−7 M and LOD of 1.00 × 10−7 M. In addition, the NS-CDs as fluorescent probe could selectively detect metal ions (such as, Hg2+, 1.00 × 10−8 – 1.50 × 10−3 M, 1.00 × 10−7 M), antibiotics (tetracycline, 1.00 × 10−10 – 2.50 × 10−5 M, 1.00 × 10−10 M) and toxic pollutant (nitrobenzene, 5.00 × 10−7 to 1.00 × 10−3 gL−1, 5.00 × 10−7 gL−1) with wide linear range and satisfactory detection limits.

All reagents and chemicals were purchased from Sigma–Aldrich and Beijing Chemical Reagent (Beijing, China), and they were used as received. Hg2+ and Ag+ standard solutions were obtained by diluting the mercury and silver standard solutions (1000μgmL−1 in 1.0M HNO3 solution). H2O2, GSH, TC, EH and NB standard solutions were prepared from the analytical reagents. Deionized water was produced through a Millipore water purification system (P18 MX, Milli-Q, Millipore).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were taken on a FEI/Philips Tecnai G2 F20 TWIN TEM. UV–visible spectrum was recorded using a Lambda 750 (Perking Elmer) spectrophotometer. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained with a FT-IR spectrometer (Spectrum One, Perkin Elmer) using a standard KBr pellet technique. Fluorescence spectra were measured on a Horiba Jobin Yvon (Fluoro Max-4) Luminescence Spectrometer. Fluorescent microscope images of samples were obtained with optical fluorescent microscope (Leica DM4000). The X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were obtained using a KRATOS Axis ultra-DLD X-ray photoelectron spectrometer with a monochromatised Mg Kα X-ray (hv=1283.3eV). XPS samples were prepared by dropping the prepared material into an indium support.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of various functional groups of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The various functional groups of CDs were synthesized through hydrothermal treatment of citric acid monohydrate and L-cysteine. In general, 2g of citric acid monohydrate was dissolved into 80mL deionized water to form a clear solution, then 2g L-cysteine was added to the above solution and stirred for a while to form a homogeneous solution. After that, the mixture was transferred into the high pressure reactor and bubbled with N2 for 30min to fully remove the dissolved oxygen. Then the reaction was kept at 80°C for 72h. After being cooled down to room temperature, yellowish-brown solution was obtained. The as-prepared sample was collected by removing the large particles through filtering with 0.22µm membranes. To completely remove small organic molecules and salts, the filtrate was subjected to dialyze in a dialysis bag (500–1000 MWCO), until the pH of dialysate turned to be 7. The obtained CDs possess various functional groups (-COOH, -OH, -SH, -NH2, etc) on their surfaces, and were written as NS-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calculation of the fluorescence quantum yield
ce:section.ce:para:
The quantum yield (QY) of NS-CDs was measured using quinine sulfate in 0.10M H2SO4 solution as the standard. The absorbance was measured on a Perkin Elmer UV–vis spectrophotometer (Lambda 750). Absolute values were calculated according to the following equation:
Q =
Q
R
m
m
R
n
2
n
R
2
where, Q represents quantum yield, subscript R refers to the reference fluorophore (quinine sulfate), m refers to the slope of integrated fluorescence intensity versus absorbance, and n is the refractive index (1.333 for water at room temperature). In order to minimize the re-absorption effect, absorbance value of the individual solution was kept below 0.10 at the excitation wavelength.

All the detections were performed under the optimal detection condition (25°C, pH = 7.0 and the incubation time was 20min), as shown in Table S2. For H2O2 sensing, adding different concentrations of H2O2 into 2.5mL NS-CDs solution (200μgmL−1, the total volume was 3mL). In the case of GSH detecting, GSH with different concentrations was added into the mixture of NS-CDs (200μgmL−1, 2.5mL) and H2O2 (2.45mM, 0.5mL) system. In a typical run, 30mL of NS-CDs were added into 10mL of 0.01M phosphate buffer solution (PBS, pH = 7.0), followed by the addition of a calculated amount of Hg2+, Ag+, TC, EH. For the detection of nitrobenzene (NB), a certain concentration of NB was dissolved in ethanol. Then, a calculated amount of NB was added into NS-CDs solution. After that, the fluorescence measurement was carried out on a Horiba Jobin Yvon (Fluoro Max-4) luminescence spectrometer.

Fig. 1a shows the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of the formed NS-CDs, which are fairly uniform and well dispersed. Two hundred NS-CQDs were randomly selected to measure the particle size. The size distribution of NS-CDs shown in Fig. 1a suggests that the particle size is in the range of 3–8nm. As shown in Fig. 1b, the HRTEM image clearly shows the lattice fringes of NS-CDs with an interplanar spacing of 0.21nm, which matches well with the (101) plane of graphitic carbon. [27] The UV–vis absorption spectrum of NS-CDs (black line) is shown in Fig. 1c. The main absorption band at 225–300nm is attributed to π-π* transitions of the aromatic sp2 in the NS-CDs [28] and another obvious absorption peak located at about 345nm is related to the trapping of excited state energy by the surface states. [28,29] The FT-IR spectrum of NS-CDs (red line in Fig. 1c) confirms the existence of free thiol group (-SH, 2565cm−1) in the NS-CDs. The characteristic absorption bands of N–H and O–H stretching vibrations are measured at 3252.4 and 3451.8cm−1, and an absorption peak at 1685cm−1 represents C˭O. Meanwhile, the absorption bands at 1195, 1245 and 1617cm−1 are attributed to C-S, C-N and C˭C, respectively. [28,30–32] The FT-IR results indicate the surfaces of NS-CDs are full of various functional groups.

As shown in Fig. 1d, the inserted digital photos of NS-CDs indicate the NS-CDs solution can emit bright blue-green light under UV lamp (365nm), and the QY of NS-CDs was calculated about 38.9% using quinine sulfate (0.10mol / L H2SO4 as solvent; QY = 0.54) as a reference. [33] This high bright light is strong enough to be seen with the naked eyes and can be used as an ‘invisible’ ink for information encryption (Fig. S1).

In order to determine the composition and chemical states of elements in NS-CDs, XPS analysis was carried out, as shown in Fig. 2. The partial XPS spectra of C1s (Fig. 2b) can be deconvoluted into three component peaks with binding energies at 283.72, 285.12, and 287.67eV, which are attributed to the C˭C, C–N/C–S, and C˭O bonds, respectively, While the N1s core level peak can be resolved into two components centered at 397.94 and 399.47eV (Fig. 2c), representing pyridinic N and pyrrolic N. The high-resolution S2p spectra are shown in Fig. 2d, in which the two peaks at binding energies of around 163.45 and 164.67eV can be identified as the spin orbit coupling positions of 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 for C-S-H. [34] These results are consistent with the FT-IR results.

In the following experiments, the effects of pH and ionic strength on the fluorescence intensity of NS-CDs were investigated. As can be seen from Fig. 3a, the fluorescence intensity of NS-CDs had no obvious change in the pH range from 3 to 10, suggesting that pH has little influence on the fluorescence intensity of NS-CDs. In order to study the effect of ionic strength on the fluorescence intensity of NS-CDs, different concentrations of NaCl aqueous solution were added into the NS-CDs solution (Fig. 3b). The result indicated that no apparent variation of fluorescence intensity of NS-CDs was observed even in high ionic strength (1.95mol / L NaCl solution) [35]. According to the above discussion, it can be concluded that NS-CDs can keep stable even under strong acid or alkaline and high ionic strength environment, which make it to be a dominant probe for versatile sensing applications.

Long-term stability measurement was also performed over a three day period in Fig. S4a. As can be seen, the fluorescence intensity of NS-CDs has no obvious change over three days, suggesting that NS-CDs exhibit excellent stability. Fig. S4b shows the normalized fluorescence intensity of NS-CDs in the presence of 12mM H2O2 against time, displaying after adding 12mM H2O2, the fluorescence intensity of NS-CDs dramatically decrease and then maintain the lowest value after 20min. The results indicate that the optimal incubation time was around 20min. Other analytes were also tested and the optimization incubation times are listed in Table S1. So, all the following detections were performed under the optimal conditions (incubation time was 20min).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of H2O2 with fluorescence quenching and detection of Glutathione with fluorescence enhancement
ce:section.ce:para: The formed NS-CDs are susceptible to be oxidized to disulfide, as thiol groups are reducing group. If adding a certain concentration of H2O2 into NS-CDs solution, the significant fluorescence quenching of NS-CDs will be observed because of the oxidation by H2O2. Based on this assumption, we used the NS-CDs for detection of H2O2 by fluorescence quenching. Fig. 4a and b show that the PL intensity of NS-CDs was quenched under high concentrations of H2O2, and the linear regression equation was I/I0 = −48.54CH2O2 + 0.68 (R2 = 0.993), and the detection range was from 12.00 to 2.00 × 10−5 mM. As shown in Fig. 4c and d, the PL intensity of NS-CDs was also quenched under low concentrations of H2O2 and another regression equation was I/I0 = −3.43CH2O2 + 0.89 (R2 = 0.995), and its detection range was from 8.80 × 10−3 to 2.00nmolL−1. Therefore, the total detection range of H2O2 is from 12.00 × 10−3 to 8.80 × 10–12 M with the detection limit of 0.001nM. The results are much more sensitive than those of sensor systems reported before, which was illustrated in Table S4. The lower detection limit and wider detection range proved that the NS-CDs are excellent candidates for fabrication of PL biosensors in detection of H2O2.

Glutathione (GSH) is an important antioxidant in organism that can prevent damage from reactive oxygen species including free radicals, peroxides and heavy metals on the basis of the reducibility of thiol group. [36,37] Based on its reducibility, we speculate that glutathione may reduce disulfide bond to cysteine by serving as an electron donor. After donating an electron, glutathione itself becomes active and readily reacts with another reactive glutathione to form glutathione disulfide (GSSG). [36–38] In the following experiment, a novel biosensor based on H2O2-induced fluorescent quenching of NS-CDs was developed for GSH determination. Fig. 5a demonstrates the fluorescence emission spectra of NS-CDs/H2O2 system after being added into 0.1M GSH solution. The results show that the quenched fluorescence can be recovered in the presence of GSH (red line). However, adding equal concentration of GSH directly into NS-CDs made little change (blue line). Fig. 5b reveals the white light and UV (365nm) radiation digital photographs of NS-CDs, NS-CDs/H2O2 (0.2mM) and NS-CDs/H2O2 (0.2mM) system mixing with 0.1μM GSH, which are perfectly agrees with our hypothesis that GSH can be used as fluorescence recovery reagent.

To realize a high sensitivity to GSH, the concentration of H2O2 should be as low as possible. However, if the concentration of H2O2 is too low, the background fluorescence signal would be too strong. Therefore, it is essential to choose an appropriate concentration of H2O2 for the fluorescent sensing of GSH. Here, we chose 2mM GSH as an example to optimize the concentration of H2O2. We found that an appropriate background signal and fairly high sensitivity was achieved when the concentration of H2O2 was 3mM (Fig. S2). In order to test the ability of quantitative detection of GSH, the fluorescence response of NS-CDs/H2O2 system in the presence of different concentration GSH was investigated. Fig. 5c and d show the fluorescence intensities of NS-CDs/H2O2 system were recovered under different concentrations of GSH from 2.00 × 10−3 to 1.00 × 10−7 M. The linear regression equation is I/I0 = −0.13CGSH + 1.21 with a correlation coefficient of 0.995. The detection limit is as low as 1.00 × 10−7 M based on the definition of three times, which is slightly higher than that of IrO2–Hemin–TiO2 NWs, showing excellent sensing performance for the detection of GSH in buffers, with a relatively low potential, high selectivity and wide detection range [39]. In addition, it is worth mentioning that the degree of fluorescence recovery deviates from linear relation to the concentration of GSH. This phenomenon may be related to the nucleophilic attack of GSH on the active-site disulfide and the formation of a mixed disulfide [37–42].

A possible reaction mechanism ( Fig. 6) was proposed showing that the thiol groups on the NS-CDs can be oxidized by H2O2 to produce intramolecular disulfide via thiol–disulfide exchange. [43] The high resolution S 2p peaks of the NS-CDs upon adding a certain concentration of H2O2 was recorded in Fig. S3, indicating disulfide was formed upon adding H2O2 into NS-CDs. When adding a certain concentration of GSH into the quenching system, the nucleophilic GSH attack on the active-site disulfide to form a mixed disulfide. [44] Hence, the fluorescence recovery could be observed. However, if GSH is excess, nucleophilic attack to another GSH molecule on the mixed disulfide yield the reduced active site and GSSG. The reduced intermediate is unstable and can be oxidized by GSSG to form disulfide again, [45] which can result in the fluorescence re-quenching. This is probably why the dilute concentration of GSH can achieve a higher fluorescence recovery.

Under the optimal conditions, the specificity influence of metal ions on the fluorescence intensity of NS-CDs is shown in Fig. 7a. The result indicates that these potential interfering ions exhibit tiny influence on H2O2 detection, which further demonstrates that the obtained PL sensor based on NS-CDs can be applied as a selective and reliable fluorescence probe for high sensitive H2O2 monitoring. In addition, titration method was carried out to evaluate the accuracy of this fluorescence method. The results (Fig. 7b) obtained from fluorescence method matched well with that of obtained from titration method, indicating that this fluorescence method can be used in the detection of H2O2. Similarly, we also detected GSH concentration by NS-CDs/H2O2 system and HPLC. As shown in Fig. 7c, two different detection methods could obtain the similar results, indicating this fluorescence method can be applied in detection of GSH in reality.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detections of heavy metal ions (Hg2+, Ag+)
ce:section.ce:para: Generally, thiol functional groups can interact with heavy metal ions to form chelate compounds based on the strong chelation [42]. For example, Hg2+ has a strong affinity with thiol group in proteins and consequently causes many health problems. Therefore, NS-CDs provide a potential possibility to be used as fluorescent probe for Hg2+ detection. As the decrease of thiol functional groups coordinated with metal ions, the fluorescence intensity of NS-CDs will decline gradually. In the following experiment, we demonstrated that this type of NS-CDs could realize the detection of Hg2+ ions. Fig. 8a displays the fluorescence spectra of NS-CDs in the presence of different concentrations of Hg2+. It can be clearly seen that the fluorescence intensities of NS-CDs were dramatically reduced upon adding a certain concentration of Hg2+. The ratio I/I0 was proportional to the Hg2+ concentration with the linear regression equation being I/I0 = −0.06CHg2+ + 0.97. The detection limit for Hg2+ was 1.00 ×10−7 M, and the detection range was from 1.00 × 10−8 to 1.50 × 10−3 M with a correlation coefficient of 0.995 (Fig. 8a and b). The similar results can be obtained for Ag+ detection with the detection limit is approximate 1.52 × 10−8 M shown in Fig. 8c and d. Compared with the silica and gold nanoparticles reported before, NS-CDs exhibit excellent sensitivity and wide linear detection range for metal ions [46,47]. More detailed comparison is shown in Table S4. Based on the above discussion, we believe that NS-CDs can be applied for heavy metal ions detection.

The interference effect of other metal ions was also explored shown in Fig. S5. Cu2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Fe3+, Pb2+, and Cd2+ were tested under the same conditions in the presence of Hg2+ and Ag+. As shown in Fig. S5, the results indicated that Hg2+ and Ag+ have more obvious intensity changes than other metal ions, suggesting such kind NS-CDs performed more sensitively to Hg2+ and Ag+.

Nitrobenzene (NB), a highly toxic, carcinogenic, recalcitrant compound, is very difficult to be degraded due to its stable chemical property. The NB with electron-deficient property can obtain an electron from excited ligand, which has been confirmed by molecular orbital theory [48]. Considering the fact that electron-transfer was likely to occur between the NB and biothiols, the electrons travel from the electron donor biothiols to the electron acceptor NB. It is necessary for us to apply these NS-CDs for NB testing. Next, we demonstrated that these fluorescence NS-CDs can be used to detect NB. Fig. 9a shows the fluorescence spectra of NS-CDs in ethanol upon adding a series of concentrations of NB. Apparently, NB has an obvious quenching influence on fluorescence intensity. Fig. 9b displays the relationship between I/I0 and NB concentration with a linear regression equation being I/I0 = −0.11CNB + 0.95. The detection limit for NB was 5.00 × 10−4 mg / L−1, and the detection range was from 5.00 × 10−4 to 1.00mg / L−1 with a correlation coefficient of 0.995. The results, however, are not good as that of current sensor systems, such as luminescent MOF (detection limit 50ppm) [49] and meso-porous carbon materials (detection limit 6.61nM) [50] in Table S4. The luminescence quenching mechanism can be attributed to the photoinduced electron-transfer between NS-CDs and NB.

Subsequently, we applied this type of NS-CDs for sensitively detecting of tetracycline (TC) based on the mechanism that the new bonds are formed between the surface organic groups of TC and NS-CDs. Furthermore, the fluorescence curve has been established upon the optimal conditions. Specifically, the fluorescent intensity drops quickly with the concentration of TC changing from 1.00 × 10−10 to 2.50 × 10−5 M ( Fig. 10b). As depicted in Fig. 10a, the fluorescence intensity of NS-CDs was dropped dramatically with increasing concentration of TC. Moreover, I/I0 was in the linear relationship along with concentrations of TC, and the related linear regression equation is I/I0=−0.27CTC + 0.87 with a correlation coefficient of 0.995, indicating the favourable precision of this fluorescent probe. The performance is superior to many other researches like Enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [51] and biotinylated ssDNA aptamer, [52] which can be observed in Table S4. In addition, we tried this type of fluorescent probe for other antibiotic detection, such as enrofloxacin hydrochloride (EH). Under the optimized conditions, the fluorescence spectra for NS-CDs were obtained in the absence and presence of EH. Fig. 10c shows that the fluorescence intensity is enhanced in the presence of EH. The ratio I/I0 was proportional to the EH concentration with the linear regression equation being I/I0 = 5.80CEH + 6.97 with a correlation coefficient of 0.995, as shown in Fig. 10d. The limit of detection for EH is 1.00 × 10−9 M, which is as good as the result of enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (limit of detection 1 ppb). [53] The possible fluorescence enhancement mechanism is that the full hydrophilic groups (hydroxyl and carboxylic groups) on surface of NS-CDs interact with surface groups of EH to form strong hydrogen-bond interactions. As a consequence, fluorescence enhancement happened because of the existence of strong hydrogen-bonds between the surface groups of NS-CDs and EH.

For various analytes sensing, 10mL of a series of standard solutions were added into 30mL NS-CDs. The mixtures were incubated for 20min before fluorescence measurement. More detailed information is listed in Table S3, as can be seen that the concentrations tested by our NS-CDs probes are similar to standard values, which confirm that the designed NS-CDs show superior precision and high sensitivity.

We demonstrate that the designed NS-CDs exhibit several charming features, including high quantum yield, strong stability and unique excitation-independent fluorescence emission. Such adaptive multifunction CDs can be used in a wide range of domains and in a variety of detection applications, including H2O2, GSH, heavy metal ion, antibiotics and poisonous organic pollutants. In each explored application, NS-CDs have a satisfactory performance to quantitative detection of molecules with impressive detection limit and detection range. At present stage, the specificity of these NS-CDs still requires improvements. In the future work, we will devote to studying different functional groups modified CDs and its effect on the specificity for detecting applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the Collaborative Innovation Center of Suzhou Nano Science and Technology, the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51422207, 51572179, 21471106, 21501126), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20161216) and a project funded by the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.talanta.2017.05.067.

ce:section.ce:para:
